era_context: Continuation of the late Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin or his immediate successors. The Old Kingdom in Egypt (6th Dynasty) was in its final decades of decline. The Indus Valley Civilization remained in its mature, prosperous phase. This was a period of relative stability in the Near East, though the Akkadian Empire's territorial integrity was beginning to face pressure from external groups (Gutians, Amorites) and internal administrative strain.

events.title: Akkadian administrative consolidation and military campaigns
events.description: During this year, the Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin continued military campaigns to maintain control over conquered territories and suppress rebellions. Inscriptions from this period document campaigns against the Lullubi (mountain peoples of the Zagros) and efforts to secure trade routes. The empire's military apparatus was highly developed, with standing armies and a sophisticated logistics system. However, these campaigns also drained resources and may have contributed to the empire's eventual collapse.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions (Victory Stele of Naram-Sin and related texts)
Administrative cuneiform tablets from Akkadian-period sites

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's military campaigns are well documented, but specific year-by-year events are not preserved in sources. This entry represents the general character of his reign rather than a specific event in 2288 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Akkadian consolidation sequence

events.title: Harappan trade networks and urban administration
events.description: The Harappan Civilization maintained extensive trade networks during this period, with evidence of commerce extending to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf (Dilmun), and Central Asia. Urban centers were administered through a standardized system of weights, measures, and possibly a bureaucratic hierarchy, though the absence of deciphered administrative texts limits our understanding. Craft production, particularly of seals, pottery, and textiles, was highly organized and distributed across multiple production centers.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of trade goods (Indus seals found in Mesopotamia, Mesopotamian goods in Indus sites)
Indus seals with standardized iconography and script

events.certainty_note: The existence and extent of Harappan trade networks are well established through archaeological evidence. Specific events in 2288 BCE are not documented, but the general pattern of trade and administration is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 2288 BCE in any known source. The entries above represent general patterns of the era rather than documented occurrences in this particular year. This highlights the fundamental limitation of ancient chronology: we can establish broad patterns and sequences, but year-by-year precision is often illusory.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2288 BCE is essentially non-existent in terms of specific events. We know the general context (Akkadian Empire, Harappan Civilization, Egyptian decline) from broader sources, but no contemporary document or archaeological layer is specifically dated to this year. The assignment of events to this year is a scholarly convention based on chronological systems that themselves contain uncertainties of ±50–100 years or more.
